sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=54, col=28).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 5, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Pattern confirmed - clicking the purple square's magenta center advances the white diamond to that position and spawns a new purple square closer to the dark gray diamond. White diamond is now at (49,30), new purple square at (54,28), dark gray diamond at (59,27).
- Goal model: Advance the white diamond along the trail until it reaches the dark gray diamond at the end.
- Action model: Each click on the purple square's center moves the white diamond to that position and spawns a new purple square further along the trail.
- Plan: Click on the new purple square center (54, 28) to continue advancing toward the dark gray diamond.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 4 of about 22 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me analyze the current state:
- White diamond (node 19): 6 pixels, boundary [[52, 28], [54, 30], [54, 29], [53, 28], [53, 27]] - it's smaller now (6 pixels instead of 12)
- Purple square (node 20): 21 pixels, boundary [[54, 26], [54, 28], [55, 29], [57, 29], [59, 27], [57, 25], [55, 25]] - this is now very close to/overlapping with the dark gray diamond
- Magenta center (node 21): at [56, 27]
- Dark gray diamond (node 22): 8 pixels, boundary [[59, 25], [59, 26], [60, 27], [59, 28], [59, 29], [61, 27]] - it's smaller now (8 pixels instead of 12)
Interesting! The white diamond is now smaller (6 pixels), and the dark gray diamond is also smaller (8 pixels). The purple square is now at the dark gray diamond's location. The white diamond has moved to where the purple square was.
The pattern seems to be that the white diamond is getting closer to the dark gray diamond. The next click should be on the magenta center at (56, 27) to continue advancing.
Wait, looking more carefully:
- The white diamond is now at rows 52-54, which is where the purple square was before
- The purple square is now at rows 54-59, overlapping with where the dark gray diamond is
- The dark gray diamond is at rows 59-61 but is now smaller (8 pixels instead of 12)
It seems like the purple square is now at the dark gray diamond's position, and the dark gray diamond has shrunk. The next click on the magenta center (56, 27) should complete the movement, merging the white diamond with the dark gray diamond or completing the level.
Let me click on (56, 27).

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: White diamond advanced to (52-54, 27-30), now smaller (6 pixels). Purple square moved to near the dark gray diamond (now at rows 54-59). Dark gray diamond shrunk to 8 pixels. The white diamond is very close to the target.
Plan: Click on the magenta center (56, 27) to complete the final advance to the dark gray diamond.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click on the magenta center at (56, 27)
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 56, 'col': 27}])
print(f"Action result: {last_action_result}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print(f"\nKey objects after action:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'p', 'M', 'G'] and n['pixels'] <= 25:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
